title: Mum guilt: what it is, what to do with it
subtitle: Why maternal guilt is structurally different from ordinary guilt, and what that means for working with it
excerpt: Most guilt functions as a signal about something specific. Mum guilt doesn't work that way. It runs continuously and adjusts to new evidence. This reading explains the structural difference, and what to do with guilt that is chronic rather than corrective.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from the broader research on maternal guilt, the 'good mother' myth, and the sociology of intensive motherhood. No single source. Framing original to homemade.education.

practice.whenToUse: When mum guilt is running as a background state rather than pointing at something specific and changeable

content.text: Most guilt has a specific target. You did something, it was wrong, the guilt points at it. Fix the thing and the guilt drops. Mum guilt doesn't work this way. It runs as a background state and adjusts to new evidence. Fix the dinner and the guilt moves to the school run. Fix the school run and it moves to the tone of voice. There is no version of the day where the guilt fully clears.

Maternal guilt is driven less by specific failures than by an impossible standard. The 'good mother' benchmark: consistent, present, warm, patient, stimulating but not pushy, working but not at the expense of being there, having her own identity but putting the children first. It is incoherent. It cannot be met simultaneously. The guilt is the signal of that impossibility, not the signal of a specific failure.
Research on intensive motherhood finds that women who follow the script most closely report the most guilt, not the least. More effort does not produce less guilt.
What to do with guilt that doesn't clear

Ordinary guilt is worth paying attention to. It points at something that can change. Chronic mum guilt is worth questioning rather than obeying. The question to ask: is this guilt pointing at something specific and changeable? Or is it pointing at the impossibility of the standard?
If it's pointing at something specific: you were impatient in a way you can do differently, you've been consistently absent in a way that matters. Attend to it. That kind of guilt is useful.

If it's the background version, present regardless of what you do, adjusting to new targets as old ones are met. The the work is not to fix more things. The work is to put the standard itself under scrutiny.
Drawn from research on maternal guilt and the sociology of mothering standards. No single source. Framing by homemade.education.
